data.title: Gladstone Investment: Most BDCs Will Cut, But Not This Monthly Yielder
data.description:
I have been bullish on Gladstone Investment (NASDAQ:GAIN) all the way since December 2023, when I issued my first article on the stock, titled: Gladstone Investment: Why This Monthly 10% Yielder Is A Buy.

Since then, GAIN has not only delivered attractive absolute returns, but also outperformed the business development company, or BDC, market (BIZD):

Most of the returns have been accommodated by the yield-component, which at least theoretically should not imply too pronounced overvaluation now. Yet, if we look at the current yield, we will notice that GAIN is offering ~6.8% in dividend, which is meaningfully below the ~10% level when I circulated my original piece. However, the key reason for this difference is not registered price gains, but instead a reduction in supplemental distributions - i.e., a completely understandable thing given the prevailing conditions in the BDC sector.

Having said that, if we want to buy GAIN today, we have to pay a slight premium of around 8%. While this might not sound like too big a deal, in the context of how other peers are priced, we are indeed talking about a rather rich multiple. The BDC sector median P/NAV is 0.90x.

Let me now share my updated views on GAIN.

Thesis review

I will immediately start by saying that I remain bullish on GAIN despite the optically unfavorable valuation setting. Another aspect that might surprise some of my followers is that GAIN's base dividend coverage is exactly at 100%. This means that, theoretically, GAIN has no margin of safety in the system to weather the forthcoming storms from, say, lower base rates and potentially rising corporate bankruptcies.

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will quickly recognize that it is completely logical and rational to pay a premium price for GAIN. In other words, the price is justified by the underlying fundamentals, and, importantly, the dividend could be deemed sustainable.

I will give you three key reasons for this.

First, GAIN has a differentiated approach towards private credit lending. It is effectively a turnkey provided of equity and debt with a target mix of ~25% equity and 75% debt on a single transaction level. Traditionally, BDCs either do not provide equity at all or do so but in a limited fashion. Think here of Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL), Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL) and Oaktree Specialty Lending (NASDAQ:OCSL).

Currently, about 37% of GAIN’s portfolio is comprised of equity products, which is just massive and certainly one of the highest shares in the entire BDC sector. And here, I can give you two important messages:

  1. Even with two-thirds of the portfolio located outside high-yield private credit investments, GAIN is able to cover the dividend. This is possible because many of GAIN's equity investments produce cash (i.e., pay dividends), and the remaining chunk of portfolio is underwritten at quite rich yields (~13%).
  2. More importantly, since the equity investments are structured from the start and backed by strong cash flows, GAIN is in an excellent spot to actually benefit from the lower base rates. In the lower base rate environments, the M&A markets tend to open up, creating more fruitful conditions for GAIN to monetize the existing equity stakes, which could bring in negatively correlated returns to falling loan income streams.

Second, from the financial risk perspective, GAIN is also strongly positioned. As of Q2, 2025, GAIN had a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.06x, which is below the sector average of 1.20x. This is actually another point of positive surprise. It is not that often that overcapitalized BDCs with notable exposure to equity products can cover their base dividends with the NII generation levels. Plus, the conservative capital structure indicates that GAIN definitely has an embedded potential to expand the portfolio base further, which is certainly positive to the NII per share generation and NAV stability. This is what GAIN's historical track record looks like (including an indication of Q1, 2026 development, which corresponds to Q2, 2025 calendar year).

Third, you can correct me if I am wrong, but, to be honest, I have not noticed another BDC except for GAIN that has absolutely no PIK income. The absence of PIK income implies that the base dividend coverage is supported by pure cash. For example, such peers as Golub Capital BDC (NASDAQ:GBDC), Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. (NYSE:NCDL) and Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) with base dividend coverage levels of ~100% have actually deficit cash dividend funding situations due to the non-cash producing PIK streams. So, in order to close this gap, they have to tap into credit facilities or simply utilize loan principal repayment cash inflows that ultimately impair NAV and/or capital structures.

The bottom line

All in all, paying a premium of ~8% is acceptable here. In my view, the key competitive advantage of GAIN is its exposure to high-quality equity investments at a notable scale. This forms a similar equity story as for Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) and Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS), which play in the lower middle market space, focusing also on already strongly cash-flowing company equity participation - just like GAIN. But in GAIN's case, the equity bias is even more pronounced, which comes at the expense of smaller income streams. Yet, given that they are still nicely backed by pure cash generation and there is an embedded firepower to grow the book, my expectation is that we will see a continued stability in the coverage despite lower base rates.

In a nutshell, GAIN has paid ~240 consecutive monthly distributions to its shareholders, and this time will, in my view, not be different.

So, I would recommend conservative BDC investors to consider GAIN for their BDC portfolio income strengthening and overall diversification purposes.

data.title: Amazon's Growth Spurt Is Heating Up
data.description:
As a long-term holder, I wouldn’t blame you for calling me biased… but I still see Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) as one of the most compelling long-term investment opportunities today, despite having what might seem like a “rich” valuation.

At the moment, AMZN stock trades at ~$231 per share and is up nearly 24% over the last year and 54% from five years ago. Today, I view Amazon as being in the early stages of several new and exciting growth catalysts that I believe will continue to reward long-term investors.

In this analysis, I’ll defend and reaffirm my Strong Buy rating on Amazon.

AI That's Already Generating Revenue

What I consider the most immediate potential growth driver is Amazon’s AI comeback through Alexa+ and AWS Bedrock.

The company has recently introduced Alexa+, with a pricing twist. They’re charging about $20 per month for non-Prime members, while it’s completely free for Prime members. But why would Amazon give away something they could charge for? Because it strengthens the Prime ecosystem as it’s worth more than the immediate subscription revenue.

This also means a much stronger moat around the membership, which already has over 200 million subscribers globally. With this reach, nurturing the existing customer base makes more sense than acquiring new ones.

The new Alexa+ is embedded with what Amazon calls agentic capabilities. So it’s no longer the same old device that you can ask to play music or check the weather. With agentic features, Alexa+ is said to be capable of completing specific tasks on your behalf, such as booking flights or adding schedules to your calendar, orchestrating thousands of services and devices to meet your needs.

On the investment front, Amazon has poured $8 billion into Anthropic, one of the leading AI research companies, which gives Amazon a significant edge in AI technology. And with a planned $100 billion CapEx for 2025, the company’s been investing heavily in AI. Should this move in the right direction, Amazon could be the next major computing platform.

That said, I believe many investors are missing the true potential of this investment. Amazon is essentially building the infrastructure for the entire AI economy, and when businesses require computing power, where do you think they’ll turn to? Computing platforms. AWS, for example, is already seeing this benefit, with AWS segment revenue increasing 17.5% to $30.9 billion in Q2 ‘25, thanks to AI workload adoption.

AWS Bedrock also deserves attention, as it gives investors an idea of how Amazon is approaching enterprise AI. Bedrock provides access to multiple foundation models, such as Amazon’s own Nova and models from Anthropic, which offer a much more flexible approach than forcing clients to use a single AI model. An LLM that’s great at writing code might not be the best for analyzing financial data.

Project Kuiper Satellite Opportunity

Project Kuiper, its proposed satellite network constellation, I think is perhaps Amazon’s most ambitious project yet. When you think of such a business model, there’s one company that comes to mind: SpaceX’s Starlink. Today, Amazon is quietly building its own low-orbit network, and it could become the next multi-billion-dollar business as global connectivity catches up to advancements. Nevertheless, the project seems to be under the radar of many investors.

Amazon aims to deploy 3,236 satellites in more than 80 launches, with a full-scale deployment that began in April 2025. According to recent data, Amazon has sent over 100 satellites into orbit and expects more than 200 by the end of the year, which means the execution appears ahead of schedule. Once the first 578 satellites are deployed, commercialization will commence, which could happen as soon as mid-2026, given the current deployment rate.

At the same time, Bank of America projected that Project Kuiper’s revenue would reach $7.1 billion by 2032, assuming a 30% market share. I think that’s rather conservative when considering the potential multiple revenue streams available. It’s no longer an internet connection for everyday consumers like us—Kuiper can be used in maritime settings, where vessels constantly need internet connectivity, or in airline settings, where passenger Wi-Fi has become a necessity.

Speaking of which, JetBlue has already signed up as the first airline customer of Project Kuiper just this month, with services expected to begin in 2027, and that’s just the beginning.

Another facet that caught my attention about Kuiper is its integration with AWS. Essentially, Amazon will offer private Internet connectivity into AWS without routing through a public network. It may not be a big deal for everyday users like us, but enterprise customers who are concerned about security and performance could significantly benefit from this approach.

For instance, some Australian mines are utilizing driverless trains to haul tons of minerals from remote locations, and these trains are completely autonomous and powered by AI. With Project Kuiper, mining operations in remote Australia can connect directly to AWS servers without their data ever passing through public networks.

On the consumer front, Amazon aims to produce terminals for less than $400, which tells me that its price will be set a little above that. If that is the case, the competitive pricing would make satellite internet accessible to millions of households currently underserved by traditional services. They will most likely lose money on each terminal initially, but sacrificing long-term gains to achieve long-term dominance isn’t an uncommon approach with Amazon.

And although it’s ever-present in most businesses, some worry about potential regulatory issues; however, I think the regulatory requirements work in Amazon’s favor. To maintain its FCC license, the company must launch more than half of its satellites by July 2026 and all satellites by July 2029.

The tight timelines certainly create unimaginable pressure for deployment, but on the bright side, they also serve as a tall barrier to entry for potential competitors. I mean, not many companies have the financial muscle to launch thousands of low-orbit satellites in such a short period of time.

Advertising Business

Amazon’s advertising business is also growing faster than the overall revenue numbers while generating margins that most businesses can only dream of. In Q2 ‘25, advertising revenue rose 22% to reach ~$15.7 billion. Ad margins aren’t reported, but one could easily expect it to be easily above 60-70%.

But why is Amazon’s advertising platform so valuable? Well, it comes down to data… but not just any data. You see, Amazon knows what you actually buy, not just what you browse or search for. This direct purchase data is gold for advertisers because it removes the costliest variable from advertising—the guesswork.

If you’re selling premium coffee, you should show your products to people who actually buy premium coffee, not to those who might be interested based on their search history.

Ads on Prime Video are another growth driver for Amazon. Again, there are over 200 million Prime members globally, all of whom are willing to pay for premium services. This is exactly the audience that advertisers are dreaming of.

Unlike traditional TV ads, where advertisers are throwing everything at the wall to see if anything sticks, Amazon can track how many viewers saw their ads and went on to make a purchase.

Moreover, the profit margins on advertising have surpassed those of traditional retail. Selling physical products generates single-digit margins at best after accounting for warehousing, shipping, and in many cases, returns. Advertising, on the other hand, can create margins exceeding 50% since there’s virtually no incremental cost to serving an ad.

However, I believe the real moat lies in the current regulatory environment. As privacy laws become stricter and third-party cookies disappear, first-party data becomes more valuable, which is what Amazon has in abundance. They don’t need to track consumers across the internet to find their data since they’re already shopping on Amazon's platform.

Financial Performance

All these growth initiatives need to translate into actual financial performance, so let’s see what kind of picture the numbers paint.

Compared to the same period last year, Amazon’s Q2 ‘25 revenue rose 13% to $167.70 billion. This growth might not appear spectacular by any means compared to smaller tech companies, but double-digit growth from such a massive base is remarkable.

Gross profit also increased 17% to $86.90 billion. This suggests that Amazon is successfully mixing their higher-margin business with traditional segments. The advertising business I just mentioned is one example of this shift in mix.

Meanwhile, operating income jumped ~31% to $19.17 billion, with margins expanding from 9.9% to 11.4%. Considering the company’s aggressive investment in AI infrastructure and satellite deployment, this margin expansion is truly impressive. The fact that there’s cash to fund all these massive initiatives also tells us that the core business is more profitable than these numbers indicate.

As a result of these double-digit improvements, Amazon’s net income for Q2 ’25 surged ~35% to $18.16 billion, which translates to basic EPS of $1.71 (notable increase from $1.29 of the same period last year).

So why is this crucial? When you see earnings growing nearly 3x faster than revenue, it often translates to better operating leverage, which usually only happens when a business reaches a scale where it can spread fixed costs over a larger revenue base.

Stock Performance

At the time of this publication, AMZN stock trades at ~$231 per share, up 5.57% YTD, 23.94% over the past year, and it has gained 53.97% over the last five years. That said, the company’s forward P/E ratio (GAAP) is 35.39x, which is over 76% higher than the sector median of 20.10x, and many are concerned.

But remember: Amazon isn’t a retailer or even a cloud computing company at this point. They’re a collection of ventures that are each capable of generating hundreds of billions in revenue. Project Kuiper alone could be worth the premium if execution goes according to plan.

Considering what I’m buying, I believe the premium is well-justified.

Risk: AWS Challenge

Of course, every investment comes with some risk. In Amazon’s case, it’s the potential softening of demand for AWS. Specifically, AWS margins dropped from 35.5% in Q2 ‘24 to 32.9% in Q2 ‘25, and that’s not a good indication. Moreover, AWS's 18% growth also lags behind Azure’s 34% and Google Cloud’s 32%.

Amazon’s TTM free cash flow also declined to $18.2 billion, compared to the $53 billion of the same period last year, which is primarily driven by the massive investments I previously discussed. Put simply, Amazon is spending nearly a year’s worth of AWS revenue on AI investments, which I think could be an unprecedented bet on future demands.

Sure, I’m an Amazon bull by any and all means, but if AI workloads don’t materialize as expected, or if pricing pressure from competitors prevents adequate returns, the value of these investments and growth development in the pipelines could diminish quickly.

But personally, I don’t believe that AWS's margin compression will lead to a downward spiral but more of a temporary consolidation. I mean, the company has a long history of investing heavily in new opportunities before they start paying dividends—they even have a robotics division, which I could discuss in a separate analysis.

Back in the day, industry “experts” also criticized Amazon for building fulfillment centers that hammered the margins, but those investments now serve as a moat that makes their retail dominance nearly impossible to topple today. I also see the recent AI investment following a similar pattern, but of course, the question about the potential materialization of demand remains.

As for the spending levels, I personally believe that this is necessary to maintain a competitive position in what could be the most important tech market of the next decade, which is artificial intelligence. I also think that for Amazon’s caliber, underinvesting and losing AI leadership would be far more damaging for its long-term investors than the current margin pressure, so I’m overly concerned about it.

Investment Verdict

So, where does all this leave us as small investors? I think Amazon offers a combination of growth and stability, and that the risk-reward profile favors buyers at current levels.

Sure, there will be volatility as the company goes through this growth phase and intense competition, but for those who can look past quarterly headwinds, I believe Amazon is one of the most favorable long-term opportunities in the market today.

Considering all the reasons mentioned above, including the potential risk, my Strong Buy rating for Amazon remains unchanged.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Small Cap Growth Insights Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

US Small Cap Growth equities (Russell 2000 Growth Index) rose 11.97% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming the Value Index by over 7%. Growth stocks rebounded sharply after a weak first quarter, supported by easing inflation, a pickup in risk appetite, and strong earnings. After a volatile April marked by tariff concerns, markets stabilized and rallied through May and June. Growth’s year-to-date decline narrowed to just 0.48% by quarter-end, outperforming its value counterparts both in the quarter, and the year-to-date period. Information Technology and Communication Services were the best performing sectors over the quarter.

Performance Review

The GS Small Cap Growth Insights Fund (I-share, net) returned 12.41% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index, by 45 basis points. Although challenged in June, the positive performance during May extended the Fund’s strong year-to-date performance; so far this year, the Fund has outperformed its benchmark by 63 basis points (I-share, net).

Our Themes and Trends pillar was the largest contributor to the Fund’s relative performance over 2Q25. Gains were concentrated during the month of May. Our proprietary signals focused on identifying economic linkages and complex connections between companies contributed the most, particularly within the Industrials sector.

Our Fundamental Mispricings pillar was additive to the Fund’s excess return during the second quarter of the year. Our industry rotation signals contributed positively during the period, particularly across our overweight positions within the Industrials sector. This helped offset headwinds in our relative valuation signals within the Health Care sector.

Our Sentiment Analysis pillar also contributed to excess returns during the second quarter of 2025. Despite headwinds in June, strong performance in May resulted in positive excess returns. Our suites of signals that seek to gauge investor sentiment as well as events were both additive particularly in the Industrials sector.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar detracted slightly over the quarter. Gains in May were offset by headwinds in June. Challenges at the end of the quarter primarily came from our signals evaluating the quality of company’s management. Most losses occurred within the Industrials and Health Care sectors.
